title: Ultrasonic Response of Weld Discontinuities
narrationScript: You know what can live inside a weld and which way it faces. Now we learn to interrogate it. An echo on the screen is not an answer, it is a witness, and you cross-examine it with five questions. How tall is it. Where is it, in sound path and in plotted position. How sharp is its rise and fall. How does it change as the probe moves. And does it repeat from the other side of the weld. The answers separate planar from volumetric, and defect from geometry. Begin with the planar reflectors, the dangerous ones. Lack of fusion, cracks, and incomplete penetration are mirrors. Hit a smooth mirror square and you get a tall, clean, sharp-edged signal that rises fast and falls fast as the beam sweeps across. Miss the angle by fifteen or twenty degrees and the reflection glances away. The echo can drop by more than twelve decibels, which is why a planar flaw can look huge from one position and vanish from another. A crack is a planar reflector with a rough face, so instead of one clean spike it often shows a ragged, multi-peaked envelope, and the peaks run along the time base as the probe moves, because different facets take their turn reflecting. Incomplete penetration at the root of a single vee weld has a special advantage for us. It usually forms a corner with the far surface, and a corner throws sound straight back where it came from. Root I P is often a fierce signal from both sides of the weld. Volumetric reflectors answer differently. A pore is a small smooth sphere. It scatters sound in all directions, so only a whisper returns. Porosity shows up as a cluster of low, narrow peaks that twinkle as the probe moves, and the picture looks roughly the same no matter which direction you come from. Slag sits in between. Its surface is irregular, so it gives a medium amplitude, broad, ragged echo, usually visible from both sides of the weld with comparable height, less fussy about angle than fusion flaws. Now the real Level Two skill, echo dynamics. Hold the indication and work the probe through four deliberate movements. Move it toward and away from the weld and watch the signal walk along the time base. A planar reflector rises and falls sharply over a short travel. A volumetric scatterer fades in and out gently. Move laterally, parallel to the weld, to trace length. Lack of fusion holds a steady amplitude along its run, porosity bubbles up and down at random. Orbit around the indication keeping the sound path constant. A planar flaw collapses as the incidence angle changes. A volumetric one barely cares. And swivel the probe in place. A mirror is lost with a few degrees of twist, a scatterer is not. One more witness, the tips. The extremities of a planar flaw diffract faint satellite signals, and those tip signals are the basis of height sizing in advanced work. Now the impostors. A weld has honest geometry that reflects sound. The root bead of excessive penetration returns a strong echo in the first leg, but plot it and it falls just past the weld centerline, slightly beyond the far surface, and it repeats from the other side of the weld at the same spot. A root crack or incomplete penetration plots at the centerline, at or inside the far surface. High low mismatch betrays itself by asymmetry. Strong root signal from one side, weak or absent from the other. On pipe, the counterbore taper reflects from inside the surface at a shorter sound path than the root, plotting short of the centerline. The cap can return signals in the second leg, and mode converted energy from the reinforcement can drop slow shear or surface components onto the screen at sound paths beyond anything real in the weld. The defense is always the same discipline. Calibrate the range, measure the sound path, compute depth and surface distance, and plot the reflector on a scaled cross section of the actual joint, with the actual cap and root profile in front of you. Compare both sides. Check the profile with a gauge. Geometry is repeatable, symmetric where the joint is symmetric, and it lives exactly on the surfaces. Anything that plots inside the volume, moves like a mirror, or refuses to match the joint drawing is a flaw until proven otherwise. Next lesson, we take these characterized indications to the code book and make the call.
imagePrompt: A technician kneels on a steel deck sweeping an angle-beam shear-wave probe beside a gridded weld seam, flaw detector propped in front showing a tall sharp echo, couplant bottle and scale rule at hand.

sections.heading: Five Questions for Every Echo
sections.body:
Pulse-echo weld testing lives on **specular reflection**: sound returns strongly only from surfaces it strikes near-perpendicular. So a signal on the screen is a witness to be cross-examined, not a verdict. A Level II asks five questions of every indication:

- **Amplitude** — how tall against the reference level (DAC or reference reflector)?
- **Position** — sound path, then computed depth and surface distance, plotted on a scaled joint cross-section
- **Envelope** — sharp single spike, ragged multi-peak, or broad low cluster?
- **Dynamics** — how does it behave as the probe translates, traverses, orbits, and swivels?
- **Symmetry** — does the other side of the weld see the same thing in the same place?

The pattern of answers separates **planar** reflectors (cracks, lack of fusion, incomplete penetration — the dangerous family) from **volumetric** ones (porosity, slag), and separates both from the weld's honest **geometry**: root bead, cap, mismatch, counterbore.

sections.caption: Characteristic A-scan signatures — a planar lack-of-fusion mirror versus the low multi-peaked cluster of porosity, with slag in between.

sections.heading: Planar Reflectors: Strong, Sharp, and Direction-Proud
sections.body:
A smooth planar reflector hit square returns a **tall, clean spike with fast rise and fall** as the beam sweeps across it. The same reflector 15–20° off normal glances the beam away, and the echo can drop by **more than 12 dB** — a planar flaw can dominate the screen from one probe position and vanish from another. That is the whole logic of orientation-based scan plans: a 60° beam square onto a 30° bevel for **sidewall LOF**, transverse passes for transverse cracks.

- **Lack of fusion** — smooth and planar along the fusion face: single sharp peak, strong from the side that strikes it normally, often weak from the opposite side.
- **Cracks** — planar but rough-faced: a **ragged, multi-peaked envelope** whose peaks shift ("run") along the time base as the probe moves, as successive facets take their turn reflecting. Tips diffract faint satellite signals — the basis of height sizing.
- **Incomplete penetration** at a single-V root usually forms a **corner with the far surface**, and a corner reflector throws sound straight back: expect a fierce, repeatable signal from **both** sides of the weld at the root locus.

sections.heading: Volumetric Reflectors: Low, Broad, and Indifferent
sections.body:
A pore is a small, smooth sphere: it **scatters** the beam in all directions and returns only a whisper. **Porosity** therefore shows a **cluster of low, narrow peaks** that twinkle as the probe moves, and — the key signature — the picture looks roughly the same from any approach direction or either side of the weld. A big cluster can also simply steal energy and drop everything behind it.

**Slag** sits between the families. Its surface is irregular and its shape elongated, so it returns a **medium-amplitude, broad, somewhat ragged echo**, usually visible from both sides of the weld with comparable height and far less angle-sensitive than fusion flaws. Elongation along the weld axis means a lateral (length) scan holds the signal over some distance, but the amplitude wanders where a smooth LOF plane stays steady. **Tungsten inclusions** are small and dense — typically a modest, tight reflector best confirmed by RT when the process was GTAW.

sections.caption: The four probe movements — traverse, lateral, orbit, and swivel — and how planar versus volumetric reflectors respond to each.

sections.heading: Echo Dynamics: Working the Probe
sections.body:
Once an indication is maximized, characterize it with four deliberate movements:

- **Traverse (toward/away from the weld)** — the echo walks along the time base as the beam crosses the reflector at changing depth. A planar reflector rises and falls **sharply** over a short travel; a volumetric scatterer fades gently in and out.
- **Lateral (parallel to the weld)** — traces **length**. A smooth LOF plane holds near-constant amplitude along its run; porosity fluctuates randomly; a rough crack shows running, exchanging peaks.
- **Orbit (arc around the indication at constant sound path)** — the discriminator: a planar flaw **collapses** as incidence leaves normal; a volumetric reflector barely changes.
- **Swivel (rotate the probe about its own axis)** — a mirror is lost within a few degrees of twist; a scatterer survives.

Add the **both-sides check** — scan the mirror-image position across the weld — and note **tip-diffracted** satellites at the extremities of planar flaws, the handle used for through-wall sizing in advanced (TOFD/PAUT) work. Echo dynamics cost a minute and routinely change a disposition.

sections.heading: Geometry or Defect? Root, Cap, and Mismatch
sections.body:
The weld's own profile reflects sound, and geometry echoes are the great false-call trap. The defense is **plotting**: measure sound path, compute depth and surface distance, and place the reflector on a scaled cross-section of the actual joint — then compare both sides and check the surface profile.

- **Root bead (excess penetration)** — strong first-leg signal that plots **just past the weld centerline, slightly beyond the far surface**, repeats from the other side at the same locus, and runs smoothly along the weld. A **root crack or IP** plots **at the centerline, at or inside the far surface** — that small plotting difference is the whole call, which is why range calibration and index-point accuracy matter.
- **Root concavity / underfill** — plots at the root surface on centerline; correlate with a profile gauge or the fit-up record.
- **Hi-lo mismatch** — betrayed by **asymmetry**: strong root-area signal from one side of the weld, weak or absent from the other.
- **Counterbore (pipe)** — a taper inside the far surface returning an echo at **shorter sound path than the root**, plotting short of the centerline.
- **Cap and mode conversion** — reinforcement ripple returns second-leg signals, and mode-converted energy off the cap can land at sound paths **longer than any real path in the weld**; treat late, slow signals with suspicion and plot them.

sections.caption: Root-zone discrimination — a 70° first-leg shot at a single-V root, with the A-scan showing why plotted position separates a root crack from the penetration bead.

sections.title: Plot before you call
sections.body: Codes require indications to be **evaluated**, and evaluation means position as much as amplitude. Never disposition a root-area signal from amplitude alone: verify the range calibration, plot depth and surface distance on the real joint profile, compare the response from both sides, and check the physical root/cap profile where accessible. Geometry is repeatable and lives **on** the surfaces; anything plotting **inside** the volume is a flaw until proven otherwise.

interactive.title: Root call: which echo is the penetration bead?
interactive.intro: A 70° probe interrogates the root of a 20 mm single-V butt weld in the first leg (leg ≈ 58.5 mm sound path). The screen shows several signals. Click the echo that is most likely the root penetration bead — geometry, not a defect.

regions.label: Initial pulse
regions.feedback: That is the initial pulse at zero sound path — it marks where the clock starts and says nothing about the weld.

regions.label: Low ragged cluster
regions.feedback: Low, multi-peaked, twinkling signals early in the leg are the signature of scattered porosity in the fill passes — volumetric, and nowhere near the root locus.

regions.label: Echo A — at the root locus
regions.feedback: Echo A arrives at the leg-1 sound path and plots at the weld centerline, at the far surface — prime root-defect territory. Treat it as suspected incomplete penetration or a root crack, not geometry.

regions.label: Echo B — beyond the root locus
regions.feedback: Correct. Echo B arrives several millimeters later and plots just past the weld centerline, beyond the far surface — exactly where a penetration bead reflects. Confirm by scanning from the opposite side (same locus, similar amplitude) and checking the root profile.

checkQuestions.question: Which A-scan behavior most strongly suggests a planar reflector such as lack of fusion rather than porosity?
options: A tall, sharp-edged single peak that collapses when the probe orbits the indication
A cluster of low peaks that looks the same from every direction
A signal only visible at very long sound paths
A broad, low echo unaffected by probe swivel
checkQuestions.explanation: Planar flaws are mirrors: tall and sharp at normal incidence and lost quickly as orbit or swivel changes the incidence angle. Volumetric scatterers stay modest but stable from any direction.

checkQuestions.question: Scanning laterally along the weld, an indication holds nearly constant amplitude for 30 mm with a clean, sharp envelope. The most likely reflector is:
options: Cluster porosity
Smooth planar lack of fusion running along the fusion face
A single isolated pore
Mode-converted cap noise
checkQuestions.explanation: A smooth continuous plane returns a steady echo along its length; porosity fluctuates randomly point to point, and spurious cap signals do not track coherently along the weld.

checkQuestions.question: A 60° probe on 25 mm plate shows a reflector at 58 mm sound path (cos 60° = 0.5). At what depth below the scanning surface is the reflector?
options: 29 mm — deeper than the plate, so the reading is invalid
50 mm
21 mm — the beam is in the second leg (2T − s·cosθ = 50 − 29)
checkQuestions.explanation: s·cosθ = 58 × 0.5 = 29 mm, which exceeds T = 25 mm, so the beam has reflected: depth = 2T − s·cosθ = 50 − 29 = 21 mm in leg 2.

checkQuestions.question: Which observation most supports calling a strong root-area indication geometry (penetration bead) rather than a root crack?
options: It plots exactly on the weld centerline at the far surface
It is only detectable from one side of the weld
Its amplitude exceeds the DAC reference level
It plots just past the centerline, slightly beyond the far surface, and appears from both sides at the same locus
checkQuestions.explanation: A bead hangs below the far surface past the centerline and is symmetric to both approaches; a crack or IP plots at the centerline at/inside the surface. Amplitude alone cannot make this call.

checkQuestions.question: A clean signal appears at a sound path longer than the full V-path through the weld region. The most likely explanation is:
options: A deeper flaw in a thicker part of the plate
Mode-converted or redirected energy from the cap/reinforcement arriving late — plot it and treat it as spurious until verified
The instrument gain is set too low
A second weld hidden under the first
checkQuestions.explanation: Nothing real in the examination volume can return later than the geometry allows; late signals are typically mode-converted (slower shear/surface components) or redirected energy from the cap or surface features.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §3.5 (response of discontinuities to ultrasound)
Angle-beam plotting and echo-dynamic practice per codes such as ASME Section V, Article 4 and ISO 17640 (cited generically)
Echo dynamic pattern classification as used in weld UT training (cited generically)
